items.q: What is the key difference between mitosis and meiosis?
items.correct: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid cells; meiosis produces four genetically different haploid cells (gametes).
items.misconception: Mitosis and meiosis both produce gametes; they are basically the same.
items.random_wrong: Mitosis happens in plants and meiosis happens in animals.
items.why: The similar names lead to confusion; students mix up which process produces gametes vs body cells.

items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: Does mitosis produce cells that are genetically different from the parent?
items.correct: No — mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells with the same chromosome number.
items.misconception: Mitosis produces genetically different cells, like gametes.
items.random_wrong: Mitosis halves the chromosome number in the daughter cells.
items.why: Students confuse mitosis with meiosis; meiosis makes four genetically varied haploid gametes, while mitosis makes two identical diploid cells for growth and repair.

items.q: Are stem cells only found in embryos?
items.correct: No — adult stem cells exist too, for example in bone marrow, although they can form fewer cell types than embryonic stem cells.
items.misconception: Stem cells are found only in embryos.
items.random_wrong: Stem cells are just cells that have not grown to full size yet.
items.why: Media focus on embryonic stem cells, but adults retain stem cells in tissues like bone marrow that replace blood cells throughout life.
Correct answer: 2 identical cells. “4 different cells” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Why must DNA replicate before mitosis?
items.correct: So each daughter cell gets a complete set
items.why: Correct answer: So each daughter cell gets a complete set. “To save energy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: One cell undergoes 3 rounds of mitosis. How many cells result?
items.why: Exact answer: 8 cells. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
